build_check.description: The project launches with the headless command, exits with code 0, and produces no fatal errors. Returns 0 or 1; the rest of the score is multiplied by this gate.

requirements.description: The player controls a boat with smooth 2D movement using keyboard, mouse, or both; the boat visibly turns or glides through water in response to input with acceleration or responsive directional control. Score 0 if the boat is static, teleports, or cannot be steered. Score 0.5 at most if movement exists but the harbor scene is empty, debug-like, or the boat moves without any sense of water resistance. Score 1 requires the mechanic to be presented in a visually authored context with real sprites or illustrated assets. Score 0.5 at most if the mechanic works but is represented entirely by programmatic shapes, solid-color fills, or default Godot widgets.

Collision or slowdown prevents the boat from sailing through islands, docks, harbor edges, or obstacles. The replay shows navigation meaningfully constrained by the environment. Score 0 if the boat passes through all obstacles freely. Score 0.5 at most if collision is only map-edge clamping with no interior obstacles tested. Score 1 requires the mechanic to be presented in a visually authored context with real sprites or illustrated assets. Score 0.5 at most if the mechanic works but is represented entirely by programmatic shapes, solid-color fills, or default Godot widgets.

Picking up cargo visibly changes the boat's HUD loadout or sprite and marks cargo as carried. The pickup state persists and affects the next delivery action. Score 0 if pickup is invisible or does not alter game state. Score 0.5 at most if cargo is only a label or score increment with no persistent carried state. Score 1 requires the mechanic to be presented in a visually authored context with real sprites or illustrated assets. Score 0.5 at most if the mechanic works but is represented entirely by programmatic shapes, solid-color fills, or default Godot widgets.

Delivering matching cargo to the correct customer grants coins, stars, or reputation and clears the order. Destination matching is observable — wrong destinations reject or ignore the delivery. Score 0 if deliveries are cosmetic or any destination accepts any cargo. Score 0.5 at most if delivery works but destination matching is absent or untested. Score 1 requires the mechanic to be presented in a visually authored context with real sprites or illustrated assets. Score 0.5 at most if the mechanic works but is represented entirely by programmatic shapes, solid-color fills, or default Godot widgets.

Multiple orders with timers or priorities create route-planning pressure; at least one urgent order is observable. Choosing order sequence or route matters for outcome. Score 0 if orders have no timing or priority difference. Score 0.5 at most if urgency is displayed but expiring a timer has no consequence. Score 1 requires the mechanic to be presented in a visually authored context with real sprites or illustrated assets. Score 0.5 at most if the mechanic works but is represented entirely by programmatic shapes, solid-color fills, or default Godot widgets.

A top-down harbor map is navigable with water lanes, docks, islands or obstacles, pickup points, customers, and route markers that constrain or guide play. Score 0 if the map is empty or not navigable. Score 0.5 at most if the map is sparse, mostly labels, or elements are purely decorative with no gameplay role. Score 1 requires content variants to be visually distinct with real authored art (different sprites, illustrated elements). Score 0.5 at most if variants exist but are represented by programmatic shapes, color swaps, or text labels without real asset differences.

At least three distinct customers or destinations are visible with different cargo needs, icons, colors, locations, or rewards, and the distinctions affect delivery choices. Score 0 if all deliveries go to one generic point. Score 0.5 at most if destinations differ only by name or color with no gameplay consequence. Score 1 requires content variants to be visually distinct with real authored art (different sprites, illustrated elements). Score 0.5 at most if variants exist but are represented by programmatic shapes, color swaps, or text labels without real asset differences.

At least one multi-stop, capacity, or sequencing decision is demonstrated: carrying multiple parcels, limited cargo capacity, choosing order sequence, or balancing urgent jobs. The decision affects reward, timing, or outcome. Score 0 if every delivery is isolated with no planning. Score 0.5 at most if planning is only implied by text with no observable effect. Score 1 requires content variants to be visually distinct with real authored art (different sprites, illustrated elements). Score 0.5 at most if variants exist but are represented by programmatic shapes, color swaps, or text labels without real asset differences.

An upgrade or planning screen offers at least three choices (speed, cargo capacity, tip bonus, route hints, or similar) and choosing one visibly changes later play. Score 0 if there is no upgrade or planning layer. Score 0.5 at most if upgrades are decorative or their effect is not observable in gameplay. Score 1 requires content variants to be visually distinct with real authored art (different sprites, illustrated elements). Score 0.5 at most if variants exist but are represented by programmatic shapes, color swaps, or text labels without real asset differences.

Victory and failure or low-rating result screens show deliveries, earnings, rating, missed orders, and retry/title controls. Results reflect prior delivery performance. Score 0 if results are missing. Score 0.5 at most if only one generic result exists regardless of performance. Score 1 requires content variants to be visually distinct with real authored art (different sprites, illustrated elements). Score 0.5 at most if variants exist but are represented by programmatic shapes, color swaps, or text labels without real asset differences.

A styled title screen shows the game name, Start button, harbor art, and courier boat identity before gameplay begins. Score 0 if the game starts directly in gameplay or uses raw default UI. Score 0.5 at most if a title exists but is sparse, generic, or mostly placeholder text with no themed art. Score 1 requires the information to be presented through themed, styled UI with real assets. Score 0.5 at most if readability relies on plain text, unstyled labels, or raw geometric shapes.

The flow connects title or scenario -> harbor shift -> pickup/delivery play -> upgrade/planning or result screen without restarting the executable. The delivery loop is playable end to end. Score 0 if major transitions are missing. Score 0.5 at most if transitions exist but are mostly static menu jumps with no gameplay between them. Score 1 requires the information to be presented through themed, styled UI with real assets. Score 0.5 at most if readability relies on plain text, unstyled labels, or raw geometric shapes.

The HUD shows current cargo, active orders, destination hints, earnings, and shift timer or remaining time in clear non-overlapping regions. The player can read delivery status at a glance. Score 0 if route and delivery status are hidden or absent. Score 0.5 at most if HUD exists but is sparse, overlapping, or mostly debug-style text. Score 1 requires the information to be presented through themed, styled UI with real assets. Score 0.5 at most if readability relies on plain text, unstyled labels, or raw geometric shapes.

Readability at 1280x720 is strong: boat, obstacles, cargo, customer icons, order timers, route hints, and HUD are distinguishable and do not overlap or become ambiguous. Score 0 if navigation or order state is unreadable. Score 0.5 at most if visuals are dominated by raw controls, primitive shapes, or layouts that feel assembled rather than designed. Score 1 requires the information to be presented through themed, styled UI with real assets. Score 0.5 at most if readability relies on plain text, unstyled labels, or raw geometric shapes.

The harbor art direction is coherent and cozy across title, water, docks, boat, cargo, customers, route lines, HUD, upgrades, and results. A consistent palette, typography, iconography, spacing, and visual hierarchy hold the game together at a publishable vertical-slice standard. Score 0 if mostly default UI or mismatched placeholders dominate. Score 0.5 at most if visuals are sparse or generic with no cohesive art language. Score 0.5 at most if the game uses programmatic shapes (ColorRect, solid fills, or untextured geometry) instead of real illustrated or pixel-art assets.

The harbor environment uses real sprite or tile art for water, docks, islands, and buoys — not plain ColorRect fills or default Godot primitives. Different map elements are visually distinct and recognisable. Score 0 if the environment is uniform color fills or unstyled rectangles.

HUD and UI chrome: cargo indicators, order panels, timer displays, upgrade menus, and result screens use styled themed widgets (custom panels, icon buttons, illustrated frames, or themed StyleBox), not raw unstyled Godot defaults. Numeric text uses a custom or styled font. Score 0 if primary UI elements are obviously unstyled default Godot widgets or if chrome treatments differ wildly between screens. Score 0.5 at most if the game uses programmatic shapes (ColorRect, solid fills, or untextured geometry) instead of real illustrated or pixel-art assets.

Animation and feedback are present: boat wake or ripple, pickup pop, delivery sparkle or chime visual, timer warning pulse, route marker animation, or coin/rating changes use tween, particles, or motion rather than instant snaps. Score 0 if all state changes snap silently with no animation. Score 0.5 at most if animation is minimal or limited to a single element. Score 0.5 at most if the game uses programmatic shapes (ColorRect, solid fills, or untextured geometry) instead of real illustrated or pixel-art assets.
